errors.tooLong: Too long — looks like microseconds. Truncate to 13 digits.
errors.secondsTooLarge: Value too large for seconds. Switch to Milliseconds?

description.whatIs: A Unix timestamp is the number of seconds elapsed since 1970-01-01T00:00:00Z (the Unix epoch). It is timezone-agnostic — the same number always refers to the same instant in time, regardless of where you are.
description.secMs: Most Unix tools and log files use seconds (10 digits today). JavaScript's Date.getTime(), Java's System.currentTimeMillis(), and many web APIs use milliseconds (13 digits today).
description.y2k38: Systems that store Unix seconds in a signed 32-bit integer overflow on 2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC. Modern 64-bit systems are not affected.
description.tz: A timestamp itself has no timezone. The human-readable form (e.g. 2024-05-01 00:00:00) depends on which timezone you display it in. This tool always shows both Local and UTC.
